_vars.raw_problem: Design an efficient sorting algorithm for a large dataset of customer records, considering the need for frequent searches by name, email, and phone number, while minimizing memory usage and ensuring the system can handle a high volume of concurrent updates.
_vars.student_framing: [{"section_type": "Problem Statement Refinement", "content": "The problem requires designing an efficient sorting algorithm for a large dataset of customer records. The records contain attributes such as name, email, and phone number, and the system needs to support frequent searches by these attributes. Additionally, the system must minimize memory usage and handle a high volume of concurrent updates."}, {"section_type": "Functional Requirements", "content": "The sorting algorithm should have the following properties: (1) efficient search by name, email, and phone number, (2) ability to handle a large dataset, (3) minimal memory usage, and (4) support for concurrent updates. The algorithm should also ensure data consistency and integrity."}, {"section_type": "Non-Functional Requirements", "content": "The system should have a response time of less than 1 second for search queries, and it should be able to handle at least 100 concurrent updates per second. The system should also ensure a minimum of 99.9% uptime and be able to recover from failures within 1 minute."}, {"section_type": "Dataset Characteristics", "content": "The customer records dataset is expected to contain approximately 1 million records, with each record having a fixed size of 1 KB. The dataset will grow at a rate of 10% per month, and the system should be able to handle this growth without significant performance degradation."}, {"section_type": "Evaluation Metrics", "content": "The efficiency of the sorting algorithm will be evaluated based on the following metrics: (1) search time, (2) memory usage, (3) update throughput, and (4) system uptime. The algorithm will be considered efficient if it can achieve a search time of less than 1 second, memory usage of less than 10 GB, update throughput of at least 100 updates per second, and system uptime of at least 99.9%."}, {"section_type": "Constraints", "content": "The system will be implemented using a relational database management system, and the sorting algorithm should be compatible with this system. The algorithm should also be able to handle a variety of data types, including strings, integers, and dates."}]

criteria.name: Problem Definition
criteria.description: The student accurately defines the problem, identifying key elements such as input, output, and constraints.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Clearly states the problem, including all necessary parameters and constraints.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to identify key elements of the problem or misinterprets the problem statement.

criteria.name: Relevance to Sorting & Searching
criteria.description: The student demonstrates an understanding of how the problem relates to sorting and searching algorithms.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Effectively explains how sorting and searching concepts apply to the problem.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to recognize or explain the connection between the problem and sorting and searching algorithms.

criteria.name: Well-Defined Task
criteria.description: The student translates the ill-defined problem into a well-defined task with clear objectives and requirements.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Produces a well-defined task that is solvable using sorting and searching algorithms.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to create a solvable task or defines a task that is not relevant to the problem.

typical_errors: Not recognizing the relevance of sorting and searching algorithms

best_framing.base: Design an efficient sorting algorithm for a large dataset of customer records, considering the need for frequent searches by name, email, and phone number, while minimizing memory usage and ensuring the system can handle a high volume of concurrent updates.

entries.title: Dataset Size
entries.description: The dataset contains approximately 1 million customer records, with each record consisting of name, email, phone number, and additional demographic information.

entries.title: Data Distribution
entries.description: The data is uniformly distributed across different names, emails, and phone numbers, with no significant skewness that could affect the sorting algorithm's performance.

entries.title: Memory Constraint
entries.description: The system has a maximum of 16 GB of RAM available for the sorting algorithm, and the goal is to minimize memory usage while maintaining efficiency.

entries.title: Concurrent Updates
entries.description: The system expects to handle up to 100 concurrent updates per second, with each update potentially affecting the sorting order.
entries.addresses_gap: definition of 'high volume' of concurrent updates

entries.title: Search Requirements
entries.description: Frequent searches by name, email, and phone number require an average response time of less than 1 second, with a maximum response time of 2 seconds for 99% of searches.

entries.title: Sorting Efficiency
entries.description: The sorting algorithm's efficiency will be measured by its ability to sort the dataset within 10 minutes, with a maximum of 5% increase in memory usage during the sorting process.

entries.title: Search Performance
entries.description: The search performance will be evaluated based on the average response time for searches by name, email, and phone number, with a target average response time of less than 500 milliseconds.

entries.title: In-Scope Requirements
entries.description: The sorting algorithm must be designed to handle the specified dataset size, distribution, and search requirements, while minimizing memory usage and handling concurrent updates.

entries.title: Out-of-Scope Requirements
entries.description: The design of the user interface, data visualization, and other non-algorithmic aspects are outside the scope of this problem.

entries.title: Customer Records
entries.description: Customer records contain sensitive information and must be handled in accordance with relevant data protection regulations, such as GDPR or CCPA.

best_framing.coverage_notes: This framing addresses all the criteria in the provided rubric, including Problem Definition, Relevance to Sorting & Searching, and Well-Defined Task. It also covers the known internal gaps in the problem, such as dataset size and distribution, specific memory constraints, and definition of 'high volume' of concurrent updates.

criticism: The student's framing response demonstrates a good understanding of the problem, but it lacks depth in certain areas. For instance, in the 'Functional Requirements' section, the student mentions the need for 'efficient search by name, email, and phone number' but does not elaborate on what 'efficient' means in terms of specific metrics or benchmarks. Additionally, the 'Non-Functional Requirements' section provides some quantitative targets, such as a response time of less than 1 second and handling at least 100 concurrent updates per second, but it does not explain how these numbers were determined or their relevance to the overall system performance. Furthermore, the 'Dataset Characteristics' section provides some useful information about the dataset size and growth rate, but it does not discuss the potential impact of data distribution on the sorting algorithm's performance.
better_alternative: A stronger framing would delve deeper into the specifics of the problem, providing clear definitions of key terms like 'efficient' and 'high volume' of concurrent updates. It would also offer a more detailed analysis of the dataset characteristics, including potential skewness in the data distribution and its implications for the sorting algorithm. Moreover, it would establish clear, measurable objectives for the sorting algorithm's performance, such as average and maximum response times for searches, and it would discuss the trade-offs between different design choices, such as memory usage versus update throughput. By doing so, the framing would provide a more comprehensive foundation for designing an efficient sorting algorithm that meets the system's requirements.

per_criterion_scores.internal_note: The student identifies key elements of the problem but could provide more clarity on certain aspects, such as the definition of 'efficient search'.
The student demonstrates a basic understanding of how the problem relates to sorting and searching algorithms but does not explore this connection in depth.
The student translates the ill-defined problem into a task but does not fully define the objectives and requirements, lacking specific metrics and benchmarks for evaluation.

strengths: The student provides a clear and concise problem statement.
The student identifies the key attributes for searching (name, email, phone number) and recognizes the need for minimal memory usage and support for concurrent updates.
refinements_correctly_selected: The student correctly identifies the need for efficient search by name, email, and phone number.
The student recognizes the importance of minimizing memory usage and handling a high volume of concurrent updates.
refinements_missed: The student misses the opportunity to define what 'efficient search' means in terms of specific response times or throughput.
The student does not discuss the potential impact of data distribution on the sorting algorithm's performance.
unnecessary_selections: The student's inclusion of a 'response time of less than 1 second' as a non-functional requirement might be seen as somewhat arbitrary without further justification or context.
